parts.answer: Identify the following: Award one mark for each correct identification. • the continent that the UAE is in: Asia • the percentage of tourists that have never learnt about the local culture: 25 • if local time in Dubai is ahead of or behind London: ahead

Describe two tourists activities likely in desert areas. Award one mark for the correct identification of an activity and award a second mark for descriptive comment of the activity in context: • Sand buggy/jeep safari [1] guided tour/racing [1] • Sand skiing/Sandboarding [1] using skis or board on sand [1] • Bedouin nights [1] traditional desert experience [1] • Camel racing/rides [1] traditional form of transport [1] Credit all valid responses in context.

Explain two reasons why tour operators should ensure that their customers are aware of and respect local religious practices. Award one mark for the correct identification of a reason and award a second/third mark for appropriate explanatory development of the reason in context: • To minimise social cultural impacts [1] tour operators promoting responsible tourism must try and minimise impacts of tourism by educating customers about behaviour [1] tourists are more concerned about their impact, so want to travel with responsible operators, which will lead to increased sales [1] • Safety of customers [1] may be arrested/imprisoned if they act inappropriately [1] tour operators have a corporate responsibility to ensure customers are safe [1] • Good customer service [1] tour operators should ensure customers are aware as this is good customer care [1] and will improve the tour operator’s public image [1] Credit all valid responses in context.

Describe three ways that tourists can minimise their social and cultural impacts when at a destination. Award one mark for the correct identification of a way and award a second mark for descriptive comment of the way in context: • Cover up in religious sites [1] respect local customs [1] • Don’t display/flash excessive wealth [1] limit demonstration effect [1] • Research customs before travel [1] prepared/know how to behave [1] • Read and adhere to signs indicating appropriate behaviour [1] minimise cultural conflicts [1] • Learn local language/culture [1] polite [1] • Purchase local arts/crafts/cuisine [1] culture valued [1] Credit all valid responses in context. October/November Question Answer Marks

Discuss the role of National Tourist Boards in minimising negative social and cultural impacts. Indicative content: • Provide information via websites/brochures/leaflets • Develop sustainable tourism industry • Inform travel agents/tour operators • Encourage responsible tourism Level 1 (1–2 marks) Will identify up to two valid roles, providing some detail but will be mainly descriptive. Level 2 (3–4 marks) Can be awarded for an explanation of the roles of National Tourist Boards, clearly indicating how it will benefit the country/tourist. Level 3 (5–6 marks) Can be awarded for evaluative comment about the significance/importance of a particular role. Better answers will have a reasoned conclusion. National Tourist boards/organisations have a role in developing a sustainable tourism industry [L1] one way that this can be encouraged is through providing information for tourists for example a web page on appropriate behaviour when at the destination [L1]. Research shows that many tourists do not research social and religious practices prior to departure [L2] and therefore can cause offence to the local population; this highlights the need for government/ministry action [L2]. If negative social cultural impacts are actively managed the industry should be sustainable and bring long term positive benefits to the country and the population [L3] that will not damage the local culture, which is often a reason for the appeal of the destination [L3]. October/November Question Answer Marks

Identify three ways that the staff shown in photograph A are welcoming the tourists. Award one mark for each correct identification: • Help tourists disembark/come ashore • Refreshing drink • Smiling/greeting Award these responses only.

Explain two reasons why island resorts are likely to be all-inclusive. Award one mark for the correct identification of a reason and award a second mark for appropriate explanatory development of the reason in context: • Exclusivity [1] premium/5star resorts/luxury travel package [1] • No other facilities/options available [1] cannot buy other drinks/food [1] • Limited transport [1] expensive/time consuming to shop [1] Credit all valid responses in context.

Explain three likely reasons why some island resorts use seaplanes to transport their customers rather than boats. Award one mark for the correct identification of a reason and award a second mark for appropriate explanatory development of the reason in context: • Luxury service [1] 5 star/premium resorts [1] • Too far for boats [1] longer distances/time consuming [1] • Quicker [1] get to resort sooner/good service [1] • Accessibility [1] access easier by seaplane than by boat [1] Credit all valid responses in context.

Explain three reasons why staff in resorts wear uniforms. Award one mark for the correct identification of a reason and award a second mark for appropriate explanatory development of the reason in context: • Clean [1] working with food /food hygiene [1] • Easily identifiable [1] easy to ask questions [1] • Good/control staff personal appearance/first impression [1] corporate image [1] • Shows department/job role [1] easier for customers [1] Credit all valid responses in context. October/November Question Answer Marks

Discuss what is meant by the phrase “tourist destinations are amalgams”. Indicative content: • Destinations are a combination of attractions, accommodation and tourist services • Combination of products, services and experiences • Made up of elements, destination name is brand Credit all valid responses in context. Level 1 (1–2 marks) Will identify up to two valid characteristics of amalgams providing some detail within the context but will be descriptive. Level 2 (3–4 marks) Can be awarded for an explanation of how tourist destinations are amalgams. Level 3 (5–6 marks) Can be awarded for evaluative comment about the significance/importance of the way. Better answers will have a reasoned conclusion. Destinations are made up of more than one element; they are the combination of different tourism products and services [L1]. The destination name is what tourists associate with the area [L1] but when considering/choosing a destination it is the combination of tourist facilities that appeal to the tourist [L2]. When marketing and developing destinations it is this combination of elements that must be managed and encouraged [L2] in order to have a successful destination [L3]. October/November Question Answer Marks

Identify the following: Award one mark for each correct identification: • the percentage of tourists that are concerned about terror attacks when holiday planning: 35 • the percentage of tourists that would postpone their holiday in the event of a terror incident: 14 • the percentage that book their holiday online: 80

State four factors, other than terrorism, that are likely to influence a tourist’s choice of destination. Award one mark for each correct identification: • Costs • Seasonality • Facilities/accommodation/attractions • Transport method • Exchange rates • Political instability/war • Weather • Safety/crime • Natural disasters • Culture Credit all valid responses in context.

Describe three negative economic impacts that are likely in the event of a drop in tourist numbers. Award one mark for the correct identification of an impact and award a second mark for descriptive comment of the impact in context: • Loss of jobs [1] fewer tourists to cater for [1] • Less taxes [1] less wealth for country [1] • Reduction in multiplier effect [1] range of industries affected [1] Credit all valid responses in context.

Explain three ways that tourists can research the safety of destinations. Award one mark for the correct identification of a way and award a second mark for appropriate explanatory development of the way in context: • Government advice (e.g. FCO) [1] official advice on travel safety [1] • Travel agents [1] face to face/ask questions [1] • Websites [1] check latest information on safety/crime rates etc. [1] • Travel books [1] prepare for risks [1] • Tourist recommendations [1] real experiences [1] Credit all valid responses in context. October/November Question Answer Marks

Discuss how terrorism has affected global travel trends. Indicative content: • Domestic tourism more appealing • Countries of recent terrorist attacks less appealing e.g. Tunisia/Egypt/Bangladesh etc. • Switch destinations but keep travelling Credit all valid responses in context Level 1 (1–2 marks) Will identify up to two valid trends providing some detail within the context but will be mainly descriptive. Level 2 (3–4 marks) Can be awarded for an explanation clearly indicating how the trend has changed the industry. Level 3 (5–6 marks) Can be awarded for evaluative comment about the significance/importance of the way. Better answers will have a reasoned conclusion. Threat of terror attacks has encouraged more tourists to stay at home; therefore domestic tourism has seen an increase [L1]. Tourists believe themselves to be safer in their home country [L2] it is easier for them to identify potential risk therefore they feel safer at home [L2]. Families are more concerned about terror threats than younger tourists. However, families have not stopped going on holiday, just switched their destination [L1] choosing areas that are perceived to be safer [L2]. The most positive aspect for the tourism industry is that people are not being put off travelling, they are just changing their habits [L3]. October/November Question Answer Marks

State three likely sporting activities available for tourists in natural environments. Award one mark for each correct identification: • Skiing • Hiking/walking • Canoeing • Cycling/mountain biking • Wild swimming • Surfing/paddle boarding • Rock climbing Credit all valid responses in context.
State two other types of specialised tourism. Award one mark for each correct identification: • Religious • Medical • Cultural • Education • Ecotourism Credit all valid responses in context.

Explain two reasons why camping may appeal to tourists. Award one mark for the correct identification of a way and award a second mark for appropriate explanatory development of the way: • Experience nature [1] camp in the great outdoors/cook in the open air [1] • Back to basics [1] adventurous [1] • Cheaper [1] own equipment/self-catering/less luxuries [1] Credit all valid responses in context.

Explain three likely rules for tourists to follow to minimise their environmental impact. Award one mark for the correct identification of a rule and award a second mark for appropriate explanatory development of the rule in context: • Take litter home [1] don’t spoil natural environment/litter risk to animals [1] • Don’t disturb wildlife [1] disturb breeding patterns [1] • Stick to paths [1] minimise soil erosion [1] • No wild fires/fires only in safe/designated areas [1] minimise risk of fire spreading/damage [1] • Camping in designated areas [1] protect sensitive areas [1] • Respect/adhere to signs [1] control and advice to manage [1] Credit all valid responses in context. October/November Question Answer Marks

Explain one advantage and one disadvantage for a sport tourist of using public transport. Award one mark for the correct identification of a dis/advantage and award a second mark for appropriate explanatory development of the dis/advantage: Advantage • Don’t get lost [1] know route [1] • Cheaper [1] day passes [1] • Environmentally friendly [1] nature lovers as sports/adventure tourists [1] Disadvantage • Inconvenient stops [1] still need to walk to final destination [1] • Limited space for equipment [1] bikes on busses [1] • Limited service in rural areas [1] once or twice a day/have to wait [1] Credit all valid responses in context.

Assess the likely positive environmental impacts of tourism in natural environments. Indicative content: • Investment in conservation • Regeneration • Visitor management – limit negative impacts • Education – encourages good environmental awareness Credit all valid responses in context Level 1 (1–2 marks) Will identify up to two valid positive impacts, providing some detail but will be mainly descriptive. Level 2 (3–4 marks) Can be awarded for an explanation of the positive impact, clearly indicating how it is positive or beneficial. Level 3 (5–6 marks) Can be awarded for evaluative comment about the significance/importance of particular positive impact. Better answers will have a reasoned conclusion. Tourism can encourage the conservation of areas [L1].The need to protect the natural assets for tourism [L2] has the additional benefit of providing long-term conservation [L2]. In some cases if tourists are not attracted to choose the area then the need to conserve maybe overlooked [L3] perhaps to fund development in other areas [L3].
